name: Beet Kvass
subtitle: The Slavic "Blood Tonic"
blrNote: Beets in Bengaluru are vibrant and year-round. Since BLR is warmer than Kyiv, your Kvass will be ready in half the time of traditional recipes. Use Mandya organic beets for the deepest colour.

ingredients.name: Beets
substitutions: Golden beets for a milder, sweeter version

equipment.name: Cloth cover
equipment.notes: Muslin or coffee filter with rubber band

tldr: Cubed beets + salt water = a salty, earthy, deep-purple probiotic powerhouse.

steps.title: Cube, don't grate
steps.instruction: Cube the beets into 1-inch pieces. Do NOT grate them - grating releases too much sugar too fast, causing alcohol fermentation instead of lactic acid.
tips: Leave the skin on for more wild bacteria
Wear gloves unless you want pink hands for 48 hours
Choose beets that feel heavy for their size - they're fresher and juicier

steps.title: Jar it
steps.instruction: Put beets and salt in a 1.5L jar. Fill with filtered water, leaving 2cm headspace.
tips: Tap water is fine if you let it sit for 24h first to off-gas chlorine
Some add a splash of sauerkraut brine as a starter - optional but speeds things up
Use a wooden spoon to push beets down - metal can react with the brine over time
steps.checkpoint: Beets submerged, water is already turning pink

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover with cloth and rubber band. Leave on the counter at room temp. In BLR, check at 48 hours - taste daily. It should be deep purple, earthy, salty, and slightly tangy.
tips: Ready when it tastes "earthy-salty-tangy" and has a slight fizz
Don't ferment beyond 7 days or it becomes too sour and boozy
A thin white film (kahm yeast) on top is harmless - skim it off and carry on
steps.checkpoint: Deep purple, pleasantly earthy, slight effervescence

steps.title: Strain & Store
steps.instruction: Strain into bottles and refrigerate. The beets can be used for a weaker second batch - add fresh salt and water.
tips: Second batch is milder but still probiotic
Drink a small glass (100ml) on an empty stomach each morning for the best probiotic benefit

culturalContext.story: In Ukraine, Beet Kvass is the traditional base for authentic Borscht - the fermented beet liquid gives the soup its signature tangy depth that vinegar or lemon juice can never replicate.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Slavic families historically kept a "perpetual" jar of kvass, adding new beets and water to the dregs of the old batch. Some claim their kvass cultures were decades old, passed between generations.
culturalContext.significance: It is one of the simplest "tonic" ferments - drunk each morning as a liver cleanser and blood builder in Eastern European folk medicine.
culturalContext.funFact: It tastes like "salty beet tea." Not everyone loves it at first, but your gut will demand it once you start. The deep purple colour also stains everything it touches - treat it like wine.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Don't grate!
thingsToAccountFor.description: Grating beets releases sugar too fast, which feeds alcohol-producing yeasts instead of Lactobacillus. Cube only.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Staining
thingsToAccountFor.description: Beet kvass stains EVERYTHING. Work on surfaces you don't mind turning pink. Wear old clothes.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Strain the spent beet cubes, slice thin, dehydrate at 45°C for 8–10 hours.
dehydratorIntegration.result: Crunchy fermented beet chips - intensely earthy with a sour tang.
tips: Sprinkle with flakey salt before dehydrating for "beet crisps"

variations.name: Spiced Beet Kvass
variations.description: Add a cinnamon stick and 3 cloves. Warming, chai-like.

variations.name: Ginger Beet Kvass
variations.description: Add 2 inches of sliced ginger. Brighter, less earthy.

variations.name: Kanji-style
variations.description: Add ground mustard seeds and black carrots for an Indian version.
